is a [collaboratively edited](/wiki/Wiki "Wiki") multilingual [knowledge graph](/wiki/Knowledge_graph "Knowledge graph") hosted by the [Wikimedia Foundation](/wiki/Wikimedia_Foundation "Wikimedia Foundation").[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is a source of [open data](/wiki/Open_data "Open data") released under the [Creative Commons](/wiki/Creative_Commons "Creative Commons") CC0 [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain") dedication. It is for the use of both Wikimedia and external projects.[[3]](#cite_note-Wikidata-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Wikidata is a wiki powered by the software [MediaWiki](/wiki/MediaWiki "MediaWiki"), including its extension for [semi-structured data](/wiki/Semi-structured_data "Semi-structured data"), the [Wikibase](/wiki/Wikibase "Wikibase"). As of early 2025, Wikidata had 1.65 billion item statements ([semantic triples](/wiki/Semantic_triple "Semantic triple")).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

**Wikidata** is a [collaboratively edited](/wiki/Wiki "Wiki") multilingual [knowledge graph](/wiki/Knowledge_graph "Knowledge graph") hosted by the [Wikimedia Foundation](/wiki/Wikimedia_Foundation "Wikimedia Foundation").[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is a source of [open data](/wiki/Open_data "Open data") released under the [Creative Commons](/wiki/Creative_Commons "Creative Commons") CC0 [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain") dedication. It is for the use of both Wikimedia and external projects.[[3]](#cite_note-Wikidata-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Wikidata is a wiki powered by the software [MediaWiki](/wiki/MediaWiki "MediaWiki"), including its extension for [semi-structured data](/wiki/Semi-structured_data "Semi-structured data"), the [Wikibase](/wiki/Wikibase "Wikibase"). As of early 2025, Wikidata had 1.65 billion item statements ([semantic triples](/wiki/Semantic_triple "Semantic triple")).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Concept

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Concept")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Datamodel_in_Wikidata.svg/960px-Datamodel_in_Wikidata.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Datamodel_in_Wikidata.svg)

This diagram shows the most important terms used in Wikidata.

Wikidata is a [document-oriented database](/wiki/Document-oriented_database "Document-oriented database"), focusing on *items*, which represent any kind of topic, concept, or object. Each item is allocated a unique [persistent identifier](/wiki/Persistent_identifier "Persistent identifier") called its *QID*, a positive integer prefixed with the upper-case letter "Q"[[a]](#cite_note-7). This makes it possible to provide translations of the basic information describing the topic each item covers without favouring any particular language.

Some examples of items and their QIDs are [1988 Summer Olympics (Q8470)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q8470 "d:Special:EntityPage/Q8470"), [love (Q316)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q316 "d:Special:EntityPage/Q316"), [Johnny Cash (Q42775)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q42775 "d:Special:EntityPage/Q42775"), [Elvis Presley (Q303)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q303 "d:Special:EntityPage/Q303"), and [Gorilla (Q36611)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q36611 "d:Special:EntityPage/Q36611").

Item *labels* do not need to be unique. For example, there are two items named "Elvis Presley": [Elvis Presley (Q303)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q303 "d:Special:EntityPage/Q303"), which represents [the American singer and actor](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"), and [Elvis Presley (Q610926)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q610926 "d:Special:EntityPage/Q610926"), which represents his [self-titled album](/wiki/Elvis_Presley_(album) "Elvis Presley (album)"). However, the combination of a label and its *description* must be unique. To avoid ambiguity, an item's QID is hence linked to this combination.

### Main parts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Main parts")]

[![Wikidata screenshot](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/13/Wikidata_layout_Phase_I.png)](/wiki/File:Wikidata_layout_Phase_I.png)

A layout of the four main components of a phase-1 Wikidata page: the label, description, aliases, and interlanguage links

Fundamentally, an item consists of:

* An [identifier](/wiki/Identifier "Identifier") (the QID), related to a label and a description.
* Optionally, multiple aliases and some number of statements (and their properties and values).

### Statements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Statements")]

[![Wikidata screenshot](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1d/Wikidata_statements_Mars.png)](/wiki/File:Wikidata_statements_Mars.png)

Three statements from [Wikidata's item on the planet Mars](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q111 "wikidata:Q111") (Q111). Values include links to other items and to [Wikimedia Commons](/wiki/Wikimedia_Commons "Wikimedia Commons").

*Statements* are how any information known about an item is recorded in Wikidata. Formally, they consist of [key–value pairs](/wiki/Attribute%E2%80%93value_pair "Attribute–value pair"), which match a *property* (such as "author", or "publication date") with one or more entity *values* (such as "[Sir Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Sir_Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Sir Arthur Conan Doyle")" or "1902"). For example, the informal English statement "milk is white" would be encoded by a statement pairing the property [color (P462)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/P462 "d:Special:EntityPage/P462") with the value [white (Q23444)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q23444 "d:Special:EntityPage/Q23444") under the item [milk (Q8495)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q8495 "d:Special:EntityPage/Q8495").

Statements may map a property to more than one value. For example, the "occupation" property for [Marie Curie](/wiki/Marie_Curie "Marie Curie") could be linked with the values "physicist" and "chemist", to reflect the fact that she engaged in both occupations.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

Values may take on many types including other Wikidata items, strings, numbers, or media files. Properties prescribe what types of values they may be paired with. For example, the property [official website (P856)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/P856 "d:Special:EntityPage/P856") may only be paired with values of type "URL".[[8]](#cite_note-9)

Optionally, *qualifiers* can be used to refine the meaning of a statement by providing additional information. For example, a "population" statement could be modified with a qualifier such as "point in time (P585): 2011" (as its own key-value pair). Values in the statements may also be annotated with *references*, pointing to a source backing up the statement's content.[[9]](#cite_note-10) As with statements, all qualifiers and references are property–value pairs.

### Properties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Properties")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Wikidata_-_simple_statement.svg/500px-Wikidata_-_simple_statement.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikidata_-_simple_statement.svg)

Example of a simple statement consisting of one property–value pair

Each property has a numeric identifier prefixed with a capital P and a page on Wikidata with optional label, description, aliases, and statements. As such, there are properties with the sole purpose of describing other properties, such as [subproperty of (P1647)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/P1647 "d:Special:EntityPage/P1647").

Properties may also define more complex rules about their intended usage, termed *constraints*. For example, the [capital (P36)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/P36 "d:Special:EntityPage/P36") property includes a "single value constraint", reflecting the reality that (typically) territories have only one capital city. Constraints are treated as testing alerts and hints, rather than inviolable rules.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

Before a new property is created, it needs to undergo a discussion process.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

The most used property is [cites work (P2860)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/P2860 "d:Special:EntityPage/P2860"), which is used on more than 290,000,000 item pages as of November 2023.[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit)[[13]](#cite_note-14)

### Lexemes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Lexemes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/L34909_tlhIngan_wo%27_%E2%80%93_Lexeme_on_Wikidata.png/250px-L34909_tlhIngan_wo%27_%E2%80%93_Lexeme_on_Wikidata.png)](/wiki/File:L34909_tlhIngan_wo%27_%E2%80%93_Lexeme_on_Wikidata.png)

Wikidata Klingon lexeme entry for [tlhIngan wo'/  (L34909)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Lexeme:L34909 "d:Lexeme:L34909")

In [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"), a [lexeme](/wiki/Lexeme "Lexeme") is a unit of [lexical](/wiki/Lexical_semantics "Lexical semantics") meaning representing a group of words that share the same core meaning and grammatical characteristics.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) Similarly, Wikidata's *lexemes* are items with a structure that makes them more suitable to store [lexicographical](/wiki/Lexicography "Lexicography") data. Since 2016, Wikidata has supported lexicographical entries in the form of lexemes.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

In Wikidata, lexicographical entries have a different identifier from regular item entries. These entries are prefixed with the letter L, such as in the example entries for [book (L16168)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Lexeme:L16168 "d:Lexeme:L16168") and [cow (L3726)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Lexeme:L3726 "d:Lexeme:L3726"). Lexicographical entries in Wikidata can contain statements, senses, and forms.[[17]](#cite_note-18) The use of lexicographical entries in Wikidata allows for the documentation of word usage, the connection between words and items on Wikidata, word translations, and enables machine-readable lexicographical data.

In 2020, lexicographical entries on Wikidata exceeded 250,000. The language with the most lexicographical entries was [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), with a total of 101,137 lexemes, followed by [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") with 38,122 lexemes. There are over 668 languages with lexicographical entries on Wikidata.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

### Entity schemas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Entity schemas")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Bagian_Wikidata_-_Skema_Entitas.svg/330px-Bagian_Wikidata_-_Skema_Entitas.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bagian_Wikidata_-_Skema_Entitas.svg)

[Human entity schema](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/EntitySchema:E10 "wikidata:EntitySchema:E10") in Wikidata

In Wikidata, a schema is a data model that outlines the necessary attributes for a data item.[[19]](#cite_note-Werkmeister-2018-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) For instance, a data item that uses the attribute "[instance of](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Property:P31 "wikidata:Property:P31")" with the value "[human](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q5 "wikidata:Q5")" would typically include attributes such as "[place of birth](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Property:P19 "wikidata:Property:P19")," "[date of birth](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Property:P569 "wikidata:Property:P569")," ["date of death](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Property:P570 "wikidata:Property:P570")," and "[place of death](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Property:P20 "wikidata:Property:P20")."[[21]](#cite_note-22) The entity schema in Wikidata utilizes [Shape Expression](/wiki/ShEx "ShEx") (ShEx) to describe the data in Wikidata items in the form of a [Resource Description Framework](/wiki/Resource_Description_Framework "Resource Description Framework") (RDF).[[22]](#cite_note-23) The use of entity schemas in Wikidata helps address data inconsistencies and unchecked vandalism.[[19]](#cite_note-Werkmeister-2018-20)

In January 2019, development started of a new extension for MediaWiki to enable storing ShEx in a separate namespace.[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25) Entity schemas are stored with different identifiers than those used for items, properties, and lexemes. Entity schemas are stored with an "E" identifier, such as [E10](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/EntitySchema:E10 "wikidata:EntitySchema:E10") for the entity schema of human data instances and [E270](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/EntitySchema:E270 "wikidata:EntitySchema:E270") for the entity schema of building data instances. This extension has since been installed on Wikidata[[25]](#cite_note-26) and enables contributors to use ShEx for validating and describing Resource Description Framework data in items and lexemes. Any item or lexeme on Wikidata can be validated against an entity schema,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] and this makes it an important tool for quality assurance.

## Content

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Content")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Wikidata_content_2024.svg/330px-Wikidata_content_2024.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikidata_content_2024.svg)

Items for scholarly articles are the biggest part of Wikidata, followed by the collection of biographies.

Wikidata's content collections include data for biographies,[[26]](#cite_note-27) medicine,[[27]](#cite_note-28) digital humanities,[[28]](#cite_note-29) scholarly metadata through the WikiCite project.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

It includes data collections from other open projects including [Freebase](/wiki/Freebase_(database) "Freebase (database)").[[30]](#cite_note-31)

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Development")]

The creation of the project was funded by donations from the [Allen Institute for AI](/wiki/Allen_Institute_for_AI "Allen Institute for AI"), the [Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation](/wiki/Gordon_and_Betty_Moore_Foundation "Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation"), and [Google, Inc.](/wiki/Google,_Inc. "Google, Inc."), totaling [€](/wiki/Euro "Euro")1.3 million.[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33) The development of the project is mainly driven by [Wikimedia Deutschland](/wiki/Wikimedia_Deutschland "Wikimedia Deutschland") under the management of [Lydia Pintscher](/wiki/Lydia_Pintscher "Lydia Pintscher"), and was originally split into three phases:[[33]](#cite_note-34)

1. Centralising interlanguage links – links between Wikipedia articles about the same topic in different languages.
2. Providing a central place for [infobox](/wiki/Infobox "Infobox") data for all Wikipedias.
3. Creating and updating list articles based on data in Wikidata and linking to other Wikimedia sister projects, including [Meta-Wiki](/wiki/Meta-Wiki "Meta-Wiki") and the own Wikidata (interwikilinks).

### Initial rollout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Initial rollout")]

Wikidata was launched on 29 October 2012 and was the first new project of the Wikimedia Foundation since 2006.[[3]](#cite_note-Wikidata-3)[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36) At this time,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] only the centralization of language links was available. This enabled items to be created and filled with basic information: a label – a name or title, aliases – alternative terms for the label, a description, and links to articles about the topic in all the various language editions of Wikipedia (interwikipedia links).

Historically, a Wikipedia article would include a list of interlanguage links (links to articles on the same topic in other editions of Wikipedia, if they existed). Wikidata was originally a self-contained [repository](/wiki/Repository_(version_control) "Repository (version control)") of interlanguage links.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Wikipedia language editions were still not able to access Wikidata, so they needed to continue to maintain their own lists of interlanguage links.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 14 January 2013, the [Hungarian Wikipedia](/wiki/Hungarian_Wikipedia "Hungarian Wikipedia") became the first to enable the provision of interlanguage links via Wikidata.[[37]](#cite_note-38) This functionality was extended to the [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_Wikipedia "Hebrew Wikipedia") and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Wikipedia "Italian Wikipedia") Wikipedias on 30 January, to the [English Wikipedia](/wiki/English_Wikipedia "English Wikipedia") on 13 February and to all other Wikipedias on 6 March.[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42) After no consensus was reached over a proposal to restrict the removal of language links from the English Wikipedia,[[42]](#cite_note-43) they were automatically removed by [bots](/wiki/Wikipedia_bot "Wikipedia bot"). On 23 September 2013, interlanguage links went live on Wikimedia Commons.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

### Statements and data access

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Statements and data access")]

On 4 February 2013, statements were introduced to Wikidata entries. The possible values for properties were initially limited to two data types (items and images on Wikimedia Commons), with more [data types](/wiki/Data_type "Data type") (such as [coordinates](/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system") and dates) to follow later. The first new type, string, was deployed on 6 March.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

The ability for the various language editions of Wikipedia to access data from Wikidata was rolled out progressively between 27 March and 25 April 2013.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47) On 16 September 2015, Wikidata began allowing so-called *arbitrary access*, or access from a given article of a Wikipedia to the statements on Wikidata items not directly connected to it. For example, it became possible to read data about Germany from the Berlin article, which was not feasible before.[[47]](#cite_note-48) On 27 April 2016, arbitrary access was activated on Wikimedia Commons.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

According to a 2020 study, a large proportion of the data on Wikidata consists of entries imported en masse from other databases by [Internet bots](/wiki/Internet_bot "Internet bot"), which helps to "break down the walls" of [data silos](/wiki/Information_silo "Information silo").[[49]](#cite_note-50)

### Query service and other improvements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Query service and other improvements")]

On 7 September 2015, the [Wikimedia Foundation](/wiki/Wikimedia_Foundation "Wikimedia Foundation") announced the release of the **Wikidata Query Service**,[[50]](#cite_note-51) which lets users run queries on the data contained in Wikidata.[[51]](#cite_note-52) The service uses [SPARQL](/wiki/SPARQL "SPARQL") as the query language. As of November 2018, there are at least 26 different tools that allow querying the data in different ways.[[52]](#cite_note-53) It uses [Blazegraph](/wiki/Blazegraph "Blazegraph") as its [triplestore](/wiki/Triplestore "Triplestore") and [graph database](/wiki/Graph_database "Graph database").[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55)

In 2021, [Wikimedia Deutschland](/wiki/Wikimedia_Deutschland "Wikimedia Deutschland") released the Query Builder,[[55]](#cite_note-56) "a form-based query builder to allow people who don't know how to use SPARQL" to write a query.

The **Wikidata Embedding Project** was made available in October 2025. It provides a [vector-based](/wiki/Vector_space "Vector space") semantic search tool, allowing plain-language queries, and supports the [Model Context Protocol](/wiki/Model_Context_Protocol "Model Context Protocol") standard that makes the data more readily available to AI systems.[[56]](#cite_note-Brandom-2025-57) The project is a partnership between Wikimedia Deutschland, [Jina.AI](/w/index.php?title=Jina.AI&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jina.AI (page does not exist)") and [DataStax](/wiki/DataStax "DataStax"), an IBM subsidiary.[[56]](#cite_note-Brandom-2025-57)

### Logo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Logo")]

The bars on the [logo](/wiki/File:Wikidata-logo-en.svg "File:Wikidata-logo-en.svg") contain the word "WIKI" encoded in [Morse code](/wiki/Morse_code "Morse code").[[57]](#cite_note-58) It was created by Arun Ganesh and selected through community decision.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Reception")]

In November 2014, Wikidata received the Open Data Publisher Award from the [Open Data Institute](/wiki/Open_Data_Institute "Open Data Institute") "for sheer scale, and built-in openness".[[59]](#cite_note-60)

In December 2014, Google announced that it would shut down [Freebase](/wiki/Freebase_(database) "Freebase (database)") in favor of Wikidata.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

As of November 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*], Wikidata information was used in 58.4% of all English Wikipedia articles, mostly for external identifiers or coordinate locations. In aggregate, data from Wikidata is shown in 64% of all [Wikipedias](/wiki/List_of_Wikipedias "List of Wikipedias")' pages, 93% of all [Wikivoyage](/wiki/Wikivoyage "Wikivoyage") articles, 34% of all [Wikiquotes](/wiki/Wikiquote "Wikiquote")', 32% of all [Wikisources](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource")', and 27% of [Wikimedia Commons](/wiki/Wikimedia_Commons "Wikimedia Commons").[[61]](#cite_note-62)

As of December 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit), Wikidata's data was visualized by at least 20 other external tools[[62]](#cite_note-63) and over 300 papers have been published about Wikidata.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

In 2025, Wikidata was recognised as a "[digital public good](/wiki/Digital_public_goods "Digital public goods")" by the Digital Public Goods Alliance.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wikidata&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Applications")]

* Wikidata's structured dataset has been used by [virtual assistants](/wiki/Virtual_assistant "Virtual assistant") such as Apple's [Siri](/wiki/Siri "Siri") and [Amazon Alexa](/wiki/Amazon_Alexa "Amazon Alexa").[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)
* Mwnci extension can import data from Wikidata to [LibreOffice Calc](/wiki/LibreOffice_Calc "LibreOffice Calc") spreadsheets[[67]](#cite_note-68)
* KDE Itinerary – a privacy conscious open source travel assistant that uses data from Wikidata[[68]](#cite_note-69)
* [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") originally started a [frame semantic parser](/wiki/Semantic_parser "Semantic parser") project that aims to parse the information on [Wikipedia](/wiki/Wikipedia "Wikipedia") and transfer it into Wikidata by coming up with relevant statements using [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence").[[69]](#cite_note-70)
* MathQA – a mathematical [question answering](/wiki/Question_answering "Question answering") system[[70]](#cite_note-71)
* As of August 2025, Wikidata has been described as the world’s largest open-access knowledge graph.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

A systematic literature review of the uses of Wikidata in research was carried out in 2019.[[72]](#cite_note-73)
